The Big Bang theory posits that the universe originated from an extremely hot and dense singularity approximately 13.8 billion years ago. Following this initial event, space itself began to stretch, a dynamic process sometimes referred to as cosmic expansion. This stretching means the distance between any two fixed locations increases over time, not because objects move through a static void, but because the underlying fabric of space grows. As this continuous growth continues, the temperature drops and matter clumps together to eventually form galaxies and stars, allowing us to observe the legacy of this early era today.<|im_end|>

Following this initial event, space itself began to stretch, a dynamic process sometimes referred to as cosmic expansion. This stretching means the distance between any two fixed locations increases over time, not because objects move through a static void, but because the underlying fabric of space grows. As this continuous growth continues, the temperature drops and matter clumps together to eventually form galaxies and stars, allowing us to observe the legacy of this early era today.<|im_end|>
